summary: This Aunt Pythia advice column addresses four reader questions spanning identity and isolation in academia, asexual dating, finding meaning as a pure mathematician, and financially preparing to support aging immigrant parents. The column's through-line is navigating difficult life circumstances while staying true to oneself. Cathy O'Neil draws on her own experience as a self-described 'bulldozer' to counsel a WOC grad student feeling isolated, validates an asexual reader's desire for companionship while offering practical resources, gives nuanced permission to a mathematician questioning academia, and provides pragmatic guidance on eldercare planning. The advice consistently emphasizes authenticity over accommodation and practical problem-solving over rumination.
key_points: Being outspoken and calling out injustice may cause social isolation, but the people who stick around can be trusted—authenticity acts as a filter for genuine relationships
Rare relationship preferences (like asexuality) require targeted searching in specialized communities rather than hoping to find matches in general social circles
Leaving academia isn't a cure for existential dissatisfaction—some people are 'identity crisis prone' and will question their choices regardless of context
Financial planning for aging immigrant parents requires concrete research into eligibility for benefits and learning from others in similar situations
The advice rejects the premise that happiness should be the ultimate goal, linking to a previous post titled 'I don't actually want you to be happy'
money_quotes: In my darkest moments I succumb – temporarily – to the idea that I need to stop calling out intolerant and/or obnoxious behavior, that I should just sit there silently not mentioning injustice.
Truth is, people can't handle you because they're not secure enough. They don't 'know what to do with you' so they avoid you.
One great aspect of being completely over-the-top real is that other people self-select for us. The ones who stick around can be trusted.
I refuse to accept the notion that minority grad students have to be at the bottom of the food chain. I get paid too little and love my field too much to not take pride in my work.
Some people (like my husband) are made for academia and would suffer outside it, and other people (like myself) have left academia but it's not like that suddenly made them happy.
Teaching is the shit work of academia. Some of your students – most, actually – are disinterested, but some are not. They are awesome.
key_insight: Authenticity and directness may cost you superficial social acceptance, but they're ultimately more valuable because they filter for genuine relationships and self-respect.
